articles.title: Personal and banking details among customer data stolen in Origin Energy hack
articles.summary:
Hackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company says

articles.titleZh: Origin Energy 黑客攻击中客户数据中的个人和银行详细信息被盗
articles.summaryZh:
公司表示，黑客获取了澳大利亚客户的姓名、地址、出生日期、电话号码和一些银行账户详细信息

关注我们的澳大利亚新闻直播博客以获取最新动态

获取我们的突发新闻电子邮件、免费应用程序或每日新闻播客

Origin Energy 客户地址

contentParagraphs.en: Origin Energy customers’ addresses, phone numbers and partial bank account data have been accessed in a hack, the company has confirmed.The firm has 4.8m customer accounts in Australia, providing electricity, fossil gas, LPG and internet services to homes and businesses.Origin has yet to confirm which, and how many, customers had been affected. In a statement to the ASX on Thursday, it said it would tell customers once it confirmed whether they had been affected.A person claiming to be the hacker has reportedly contacted media outlets with unverified claims that 2 million customers’ details were accessed.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailOrigin said data may include customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and Origin account information, as well as the last four digits of a credit card, or the last three digits of a bank account.The company said incomplete credit card or bank account information could not be used to make purchases or access accounts.Origin first revealed the hack in a statement yesterday, saying it believed credit card or bank details had not been accessed. The company has not detailed how the hack occurred.Origin’s chief executive, Frank Calabria, said the company was securing its systems and ensuring there was no further unauthorised access, working with independent cyber experts and authorities.“I’m sorry this has happened,” Calabria said.“Customers trust Origin with their information, and I apologise for the impact this may cause.”Experts have warned customers could be vulnerable if the data is leaked and used for identity theft, or by scammers tricking people into believing they are representatives of real businesses.Rumpa Dasgupta, a lecturer in cybersecurity at La Trobe University, said personalised records could be misused for physical robberies.“In the wrong hands, this information could be exploited not only for highly targeted phishing campaigns but also to support physical crimes such as burglary by identifying vulnerable properties,” Dasgupta said.Origin said the Australian Cyber Security Centre, the Australian federal police and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OIAC) were all investigating.skip past newsletter promotionafter newsletter promotionThe National Office of Cyber Security is leading the government’s response.The AFP declined to comment on whether it had identified or communicated with Origin’s hackers.The Australian reported it was first contacted by a person claiming to have hacked Origin on Tuesday, after which the newspaper alerted Origin, which made a statement on Wednesday.An Origin spokesperson said the company had moved immediately to update the ASX as soon as it was aware of a potential incident.The OIAC reported it received 1,205 data breach notifications in 2025, of which 716 were related to malicious or criminal activity.Among the hacks was a leak of 5 million Qantas customers’ information in October, which prompted warnings scammers could cold call leaked phone numbers.The federal privacy commissioner last week found Qantas did not make any omissions or failings in breach of the Privacy Act, in relation to the hack. The Australian federal police are investigating.

articles.title: UK millionaires ask Andy Burnham to tax them more
articles.summary: Former footballer Gary Lineker and producer Brian Eno write to the new PM telling him "we can afford it".
articles.titleZh: 英国百万富翁要求安迪·伯纳姆对他们征收更多税
articles.summaryZh: 前足球运动员加里·莱因克尔和制片人布莱恩·伊诺写信给新任总理，告诉他“我们负担得起”。

contentParagraphs.en: Millionaires including former footballer Gary Lineker and music producer Brian Eno have written to new prime minister Andy Burnham asking to be taxed more.In an open letter, 120 well-off Britons told Burnham: "We can afford it. We're not talking about higher taxes on those who get up and go to work for their income every day, but on the very richest whose income is derived from the wealth they hold."Organised by Patriotic Millionaires, the letter said it would lead to a more equal society, and urged a "devolution of wealth and power from the very richest". People can already give money or stocks voluntarily to the Treasury using its donation facility.Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch said Lineker is "very welcome to pay more tax he can write a cheque to the treasury, no one is stopping him."The group supports a 2% tax on wealth over £10m."Millionaires are a patriotic bunch," the letter states. "We love this country and we want it to succeed."But success requires investment and a primary source of untouched capital investment is sitting with us, in untaxed potential."Other signatories include Richard Curtis, director of Notting Hill and Love Actually and Ian Gregg, the former managing director of Greggs and son of the bakery chain's founder.The renewed call for higher tax on the wealthy follows a similar campaign in previous years. Burnham did not rule out a wealth tax when asked about it by Lineker a few days before he became prime minister.He suggested he may have "to ask for a little more" tax at some point.The latest call from Patriotic Millionaires said there is a "need to embrace a new kind of devolution of wealth and power, from the very richest in order to reinvest back into our greatest asset in every region".It added that in its own poll, the majority of millionaires wanted a higher tax on themselves. "There are a few people left with outdated economic thinking and few others desperate to hold onto every penny they can... Those that can't see past the end of their own self interest have no place in designing a Britain for the future," the letter said.

articles.title: Iran, Houthis strike tankers as US bombing continues: What’s the latest?
articles.summary: With the Houthi naval blockade, shipping disruptions now impact the Bab al-Mandeb strait in addition to Hormuz.
articles.titleZh: 美国轰炸仍在继续，伊朗、胡塞武装袭击油轮：最新情况如何？
articles.summaryZh: 由于胡塞武装的海上封锁，除了霍尔木兹海峡外，航运中断现在还影响到曼德海峡。

contentParagraphs.en: The United States continued to hit Iran for a 12th consecutive night early on Thursday, and Iran retaliated, striking targets across the Gulf, particularly in Kuwait and Jordan.Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said on Thursday that an explosion set a tanker ablaze in the Strait of Hormuz after it attempted to navigate the southern route off the coast of Oman.Here is a recap of what happened on Wednesday night and Thursday.Where did the US attack Iran?US Central Command (CENTCOM) said at 02:30 GMT on Thursday that it concluded the latest round of strikes against Iran.It targeted Iranian military targets, including maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites and air defence assets, CENTCOM said, without specifying what areas in Iran were struck.A local official told the Mehr news agency that US missiles struck the area around a passenger terminal in Shalamcheh, a western city near the Iraqi border. Two people were killed in the attack, the deputy governor of Iran’s Khuzestan province told the state broadcaster IRIB on Thursday. Local media also reported that 11 people were injured.Al Jazeera’s Tohid Assidi reported from Iranian capital Tehran that overnight strikes targeted at least three cities in the southwestern province of Khuzestan. This included the city of Ramshir, where an asphalt facility was reported to have been targeted.In Hormozgan province, explosions were heard in the city of Sirik, which overlooks the Strait of Hormuz, Assidi reported.He added that the governor confirmed an overnight attack in Bushehr on a power substation near the city’s nuclear power plant. This resulted in a few hours of power outage in the city.IRIB also reported that explosions were heard in Iran’s Jask.The Tasnim news agency reported that a US missile hit a location on the outskirts of the city of Andimeshk.Two naval search and rescue vessels were hit and seriously damaged in a US attack on Iran’s Hormozgan, the ISNA news agency reported, citing the province’s Ports and Maritime Administration.Where did Iran hit back?KuwaitThe IRGC claimed attacks on US assets in Kuwait on Thursday.It said it had “attacked and destroyed a large warehouse of military equipment, a Patriot missile defence system, and a hangar of American MQ9 drones” at Ali Al Salem airbase.Addressing the Kuwaiti people, the IRGC insisted that it was the US, not Iran, that had “violated your territorial integrity and sovereignty”.It said its attacks were aimed at US bases, territory it described as beyond the control of Kuwait’s own border guards.“So it is America that has violated your territorial integrity and sovereignty, not us. We are attacking lands occupied by the American army, an army that knows nothing but crime,” the IRGC said.JordanLater on Thursday, the IRGC claimed attacks on US assets in Jordan, saying it had “destroyed a radar of the American THAAD missile defence system with lightning strikes”.“A Patriot system and a C-RAM radar were also targeted and destroyed, and the fuel tanks of the American base were set on fire,” it said.The IRGC added that “a large helicopter equipment warehouse and a helicopter repair and maintenance shed were also set on fire and destroyed”.Addressing the Jordanian people, it insisted that its actions did not violate Jordanian sovereignty, arguing that the US military presence infringes on local authority and that targeting US forces was a legal and necessary response.What happened in the Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea?The IRGC said an explosion set a tanker on fire in the Strait of Hormuz.In a statement carried by Iranian media, it said two other vessels quickly turned back following the explosion.The IRGC claimed the three ships were acting under US orders and had “intended to pass through the mine-laid route south of the Strait of Hormuz”.“The powerful IRGC Navy emphasises that the Strait of Hormuz is under our control and that as long as America’s evil deeds in the region continue, it is completely closed and no oil tanker will enter or exit [the strait],” it warned.“Any ship that is deceived by America and intends to pass through without coordination with the Islamic Republic of Iran will suffer the same fate.”The UK Maritime Trade Operations Centre said a ship was targeted 70 nautical miles (130km) off the Saudi coast, causing a fire on board. There was no mention of casualties among the crew.The Houthis, meanwhile, claimed a major operation against two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, called the Encelia and the Layla. The Saudi SPA news agency confirmed the Encelia was hit.The Houthis said they targeted those two tankers with cruise and ballistic missiles as well as drones, causing huge balls of fire and devastation.The Houthis framed the attack as part of a tit-for-tat campaign against Saudi Arabia. They are calling it a “siege-for-siege”. They said it has been 12 years since Saudi Arabia has been imposing a siege on the Yemeni people, and that it is now time for Yemen and the Houthis to respond in kind. Riyadh has rejected Houthi claims that it had besieged Yemen.

articles.title: A town renamed its festival 'AI Love Irondale Day.' Then came the comments
articles.summary: The online outcry over the renaming of a summer festival illustrates the unease over the data center boom in the South.
articles.titleZh: 一个小镇将其节日重新命名为“AI Love Irondale Day”。然后评论就来了
articles.summaryZh: 网上对夏季节日更名的强烈抗议表明了人们对南方数据中心繁荣的不安。

contentParagraphs.en: Festival-goers sit outside Irondale, Alabama's city hall on I Love Irondale Day, July 18, 2026.
contentParagraphs.zh: 2026年7月18日，在阿拉巴马州市政厅的艾恩代尔（ Irondale ）外，节日观众坐在“我爱艾恩代尔日” （ I Love

contentParagraphs.en: Sign up and be the first to hear about new Tiny Desk concerts and behind the scenes glimpses
contentParagraphs.zh: 注册成为第一个了解全新Tiny Desk音乐会和幕后花絮的人

contentParagraphs.en: Every summer, the I Love Irondale Day festival celebrates the small city neighboring Birmingham, Alabama, most famous as the home of the cafe that inspired the 90s dramedy Fried Green Tomatoes.
contentParagraphs.zh: 每年夏天， I Love Irondale Day节庆祝邻近阿拉巴马州伯明翰的小城市，最著名的是咖啡馆的所在地，它启发了90年代的戏剧性油炸绿西红柿。

contentParagraphs.en: But earlier this month, Mayor James D. Stewart announced on his Facebook page that the festival would be renamed AI Love Irondale Day. Get it?
contentParagraphs.zh: 但本月早些时候，市长詹姆斯· D ·斯图尔特（ James D. Stewart ）在他的Facebook页面上宣布，该节日将更名为AI Love Irondale Day。明白吗？

contentParagraphs.en: People in the comments did and hated it.
contentParagraphs.zh: 评论中的人做到了，也讨厌它。

contentParagraphs.en: Some trashed AI, calling it "absolutely garbage for the environment" and the festival an "AI slop event." Another person said the post sounded like a pep rally for what's become a boogeyman in the South: data centers.
contentParagraphs.zh: 一些被废弃的人工智能，称之为“绝对是环境的垃圾” ，而这个节日是一个“人工智能污点事件”。“另一个人说，这个帖子听起来像是一个鼓舞人心的集会，因为它已经成为南方的一个恶棍：数据中心。

contentParagraphs.en: While two city councilmembers told NPR there are no immediate plans for a data center in Irondale, nearby Bessemer, Alabama is prepping for a multibillion-dollar data center — and residents have been fighting against it.
contentParagraphs.zh: 虽然两名市议员告诉NPR ，目前还没有在阿拉巴马州贝塞默附近的Irondale建立数据中心的计划，但阿拉巴马州正在准备建设一个价值数十亿美元的数据中心，居民们一直在与之抗争。

contentParagraphs.en: In the face of the backlash, Stewart posted a new video: The rebranding was just "a play on words," he said, and the festival would not be about AI or data centers. Still, he said, the festival would return to its original name: I Love Irondale.
contentParagraphs.zh: 面对强烈反对，斯图尔特发布了一段新视频：他说，品牌重塑只是“文字游戏” ，这个节日不会是关于人工智能或数据中心的。不过，他说，这个节日将恢复原来的名字：我爱Irondale。

contentParagraphs.en: (Stewart's office did not respond to a request for comment.)
contentParagraphs.zh: （斯图尔特的办公室没有回复置评请求。）

contentParagraphs.en: Data centers are a hot topic, especially in parts of the South and the Midwest that are seeing the biggest construction boom. Local officials often herald them as a source of new jobs and tax dollars — although once construction is finished, it's not clear if they'll create many permanent jobs.
contentParagraphs.zh: 数据中心是一个热门话题，尤其是在南部和中西部地区，那里的建筑热潮最大。当地官员经常预示着他们是新工作岗位和税收的来源--尽管一旦施工完成，尚不清楚他们是否会创造许多永久性工作岗位。

contentParagraphs.en: Yet public disdain for data centers is widespread. A Gallup poll from March found that 70% of Americans are opposed to the centers being built where they live. They worry about noise pollution, and about how power-and-water-hungry data centers would affect their utility bills and the environment.
contentParagraphs.zh: 然而，公众对数据中心的蔑视是普遍存在的。盖洛普3月份的一项民意调查发现， 70%的美国人反对在他们居住的地方建造中心。他们担心噪音污染，以及耗电耗水的数据中心将如何影响他们的水电费和环境。

contentParagraphs.en: "My fear is all of the above," said Kathleen Haberstroh, who attended the city's festival on Saturday, when it went ahead under its original name. "It just feels predatory."
contentParagraphs.zh: 凯瑟琳·哈伯斯特罗（ Kathleen Haberstroh ）说： “我担心的是上述所有事情。”她周六参加了这个城市的节日，当时它以原来的名字继续前进。“只是感觉有点掠夺性。”

contentParagraphs.en: "I don't like data centers," said resident John Horton. He called them "a waste of space, resources, community."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我不喜欢数据中心，”居民John Horton说。他称之为“浪费空间、资源和社区”。

contentParagraphs.en: On the other hand, he does indeed love Irondale. "I don't want to sound corny and say there's a certain magic to it, but it's right by the railroad tracks. You've got Whistle Stop Cafe," he said. "You have that sunset. I mean there's something beautiful about it."
contentParagraphs.zh: 另一方面，他确实爱Irondale。“我不想听起来老生常谈，说它有一定的魔力，但它就在铁轨旁边。你有Whistle Stop Cafe ，”他说。“你有那个日落。我的意思是它有一些美丽的东西。”

contentParagraphs.en: Amelia Fielder also praised the city she's lived in for 26 years. However, she's not opposed to the idea of AI in Irondale and would welcome a data center neighbor, believing it would bring jobs.
contentParagraphs.zh: 阿米莉亚·菲尔德（ Amelia Fielder ）也称赞了她居住了26年的城市。然而，她并不反对在Irondale使用人工智能的想法，并且会欢迎数据中心邻居，因为她相信这会带来就业机会。

contentParagraphs.en: She also said this year's festival was the biggest yet, and she didn't have an issue with its brief renaming after AI. "Everything else is AI," said Fielder. "Why not?"
contentParagraphs.zh: 她还表示，今年的节日是迄今为止规模最大的，她对人工智能之后的短暂更名没有任何问题。“其他一切都是人工智能，”菲尔德说。“为什么不呢？”

contentParagraphs.en: City councilmember Aaron Sims, spotted at the festival, said he does not expect any huge data centers to come to Irondale.
contentParagraphs.zh: 参加音乐节的市议员亚伦·西姆斯（ Aaron Sims ）表示，他预计Irondale不会有任何大型数据中心。

contentParagraphs.en: "We don't have 150 acres just sitting out there for a big hyperscale facility," he said. But, he said, the council is creating new rules in case a developer is interested in building a smaller data center.
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们没有150英亩的土地，只是坐在那里进行大型超大规模设施，”他说。但是，他说，该委员会正在制定新的规则，以防开发商有兴趣建造一个较小的数据中心。

contentParagraphs.en: On the reason for the attempt to rename the festival, Sims said, "I think the mayor just realized that, you know, AI is here. Tech is here. We want our citizens educated on the future. This is the little taste today."
contentParagraphs.zh: 关于重命名节日的原因， Sims说： “我认为市长刚刚意识到，你知道，人工智能就在这里。科技在这里。我们希望我们的公民接受未来的教育。这就是今天的小味道。”

contentParagraphs.en: Even with AI no longer in the name, the festival did have something of a tech theme. Dancers on stilts — dressed as robots — moved to a Spanish version of "Achy Breaky Heart."
contentParagraphs.zh: 即使不再以人工智能命名，电影节也确实有一些科技主题。穿着高跷的舞者—打扮成机器人—搬到了西班牙版的“Achy Breaky Heart”。

contentParagraphs.en: A dancer on stilts is dressed as a robot -- but not doing "the robot" -- at I Love Irondale Day, July 22, 2026.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在2026年7月22日的I Love Irondale Day上，一名穿着高跷的舞者被打扮成机器人，但不做“机器人”。

contentParagraphs.en: There was a screen set up where festival-goers were supposed to answer questions to teach a chatbot about Irondale — but it was not working.
contentParagraphs.zh: 有一个屏幕设置，节日观众应该回答问题，向聊天机器人介绍Irondale —但它不起作用。

contentParagraphs.en: The festival's big tech hit was the drone show finale. Dewar Gaines watched with his son as drones gathered in the sky to form the Statue of Liberty, the American flag and spelled out "I Love Irondale."
contentParagraphs.zh: 电影节的科技热门节目是无人机节目的结局。杜瓦·盖恩斯（ Dewar Gaines ）和他的儿子看着无人机聚集在天空中形成自由女神像，美国国旗，并拼写出“我爱艾昂戴尔”。

contentParagraphs.en: Gaines, who owns a dog treat factory in Irondale, has heard firsthand how loud data centers can be and doesn't want one built near him.
contentParagraphs.zh: Gaines在Irondale拥有一家狗食工厂，他亲耳听到数据中心有多大声，也不希望数据中心建在他附近。

contentParagraphs.en: That said, he relies on AI for work. "So I guess I'm a hypocrite in that regard," said Gaines. "Because it's the most powerful tool I have."
contentParagraphs.zh: 也就是说，他依靠人工智能来工作。“所以我想我在这方面是个伪君子，”盖恩斯说。“因为这是我拥有的最强大的工具。”

articles.title: Airport drop-off fees up by a third - here are the priciest
articles.summary: The cost of parking closest to the terminal has hit £10 at two of the UK's airports.
articles.titleZh: 机场送机费上涨了三分之一 - 这是最贵的
articles.summaryZh: 在英国的两个机场，最靠近航站楼的停车费已达到 10 英镑。

contentParagraphs.en: Drop-off fees at the UK's major airports have risen by a third on average since last summer, new analysis has found.The RAC motoring group looked at the cost of pulling up to the closest point to terminals at the 20 busiest airports, 16 of which had put up their prices since last year.London airport Gatwick topped the list by charging a "farewell fee" of £10 for 10 minutes. after lifting it by £3 from last year. Stansted also charges £10 but drivers are allowed 15 minutes at its "express set down" outside departures.Airports UK, the trade body, said that free drop-offs were available and fees were part of airports' business models and climate change objectives.The RAC said that the price of dropping people off in "kiss-and-fly" areas rose by an average of £1.70 to £7 compared to this time last year. London City Airport, which only started making people pay this year, is the most expensive on a per-minute basis. Its £8 charge for five minutes works out at £1.60 per minute.Birmingham is the cheapest, charging £7.00 for 15 minutes which equates to 47p per minute. In contrast, none of the top 10 airports in the EU charge for drop-offs, according to RAC research.The RAC acknowledged UK airports generally offered other options for setting people down at locations further from the terminal, sometimes with free shuttle buses.But the motoring organisation's senior policy officer, Rod Dennis said "having a captive audience should be no excuse for these 'farewell fees' – especially when they're completely at odds with how major airports around the world operate". He said drivers needed to be especially careful of barrierless "park now, pay later" drop-off zones, as they could face a parking charge notice if they failed to pay online or by phone within a certain period of time.The Aviation Environment Federation (AEF) campaign group defended the drop-off fees.It said there needs to be an incentive for people to use other forms of transport to reduce congestion and air pollution around airports, which it said can cause a range of childhood illnesses such as asthma.Celeste Hicks, policy manager at AEF, said: "Any parking charges or drop off charges could be reinvested by laying on local bus and rail services instead of lining the pockets of airport shareholders."Airports want more people to arrive by trains and buses.When Transport Secretary Heidi Alexander approved Gatwick's plans for a second runway, one requirement was to have 54% of passengers using public transport.Heathrow may also have to act to try and put people off driving to get a third runway.A London Gatwick spokesperson said drop-off charges were aimed at disincentivising "kiss and fly" trips and data indicated it was "influencing passengers to seek alternative options".The airport links to 120 train stations across London and the south east, with eight local bus routes running 24/7, they said.Passengers can still be dropped off for free at long-stay car parks, with a free shuttle bus to the terminal and Blue Badge holders are exempt from the charge, they said.A spokesperson for Airports UK said where drop off fees are charged, they are "necessary to manage congestion, and the traffic and air pollution concerns of our local communities, as well as our climate change objectives". "These are issues that airports are mandated to manage by the government and local authorities," they said.But Clive Wratten, chief executive of the Business Travel Association, said fees were adding to businesses' costs."First it was environmental [reasons]. Then congestion management. Then funding new routes. Now apparently it is about offsetting tax rises," he said. "The goalposts have moved so many times now that the credibility of any justification has long since gone."

articles.title: River levels approach all-time low as drought announcement looms
articles.summary: An announcement is expected from NRW as some river levels are reported to be nearing all-time lows.
articles.titleZh: 随着干旱公告的临近，河流水位接近历史最低水平
articles.summaryZh: 由于据报道一些河流水位接近历史最低点，预计北威州将发布公告。

contentParagraphs.en: River levels approach all-time low as drought announcement loomsImage source, Janey Liz/Weather WatchersImage caption, Natural Resources Wales said that north and south-east Wales were moving to "prolonged dry weather" statusBySteffan MessengerWales environment correspondent and Elen DaviesBBC WalesUpdated 1 hour agoRiver levels are approaching all-time lows across Wales as an imminent "environmental drought" announcement looms for parts of the country.Wales has seen about 5% of the average rainfall for July, after a series of back-to-back heatwaves and a drier than usual April to June.Afonydd Cymru, which represents Wales' rivers trusts, said low river flows were causing "significant problems", killing fish and other wildlife, while farming unions warned of failing crops and heat-stressed livestock.An announcement is expected later on Thursday from Natural Resources Wales (NRW), who said they were closely monitoring the situation.Image source, Tudur DaviesImage caption, The river Elwy in Denbighshire is among those running lowNRW announced last week that north and south-east Wales were moving to "prolonged dry weather" status - classed as a first step towards drought.It triggered enhanced monitoring and checks of waterways and important sites for wildlife as well as advice for farmers and others who use water from rivers.Huwel Manley, NRW head of operations for south west Wales, said a number of watercourses were drying up and "even larger rivers are running at lower flows than usual".Rivers classed as exceptionally low include the Clwyd, Alwen, Alyn, Clywedog, Dyfi and Conwy in north Wales as well as the Wye, which runs through parts of mid Wales and south along the border with England.Extra water was being released from reservoirs to support flows on the Clwyd and Wye, as well as the river Severn in the south east and the Dee in north-east Wales.Image source, Tudur DaviesImage caption, Much of Wales has gone for weeks without a drop of rain While there is some rain in the forecast for the weekend, it will take time for river systems to recover, Manley warned.Parts of the country could see the declaration of an "environmental drought", he predicted.This would mean that water shortages in rivers and the ground, as well as dry soils, were having an immediate and detrimental impact on ecosystems, rather than there being a threat to drinking water or household supplies.But Manley urged the public to be careful with their use of water - for the sake of wildlife and the wider environment."Maybe have a shower rather than a bath," he said. "And then there are also little things that can be done, such as putting some water out in the garden for mammals and birds."Image source, Tudur DaviesImage caption, Despite rain in the forecast for the weekend, it will take time for river systems to recoverWhat is an environmental drought?An environmental drought occurs when a prolonged lack of rainfall and high temperatures cause severe stress on the natural ecosystem. NRW says it results in critically low river flows, diminished reservoir reserves and exceptionally low groundwater levels, negatively impacting wildlife, fisheries and protected habitats.This type of drought typically develops before agricultural impacts (like failing crops) or public water shortages occur. Impacts include:Shrunken rivers - a decrease in oxygen levels, an increase water temperatures and a restriction in the migration of species like salmonHabitat loss - reduced water levels cause riverbeds to dry up, leading to the loss of biodiversity and fish killsPrivate water pressures - natural springs and private boreholes can run dryWhen environmental drought conditions, external are identified, NRW often transitions areas to a "prolonged dry weather" status.This triggers enhanced monitoring of waterways, restricts water abstraction licenses to protect low flows and leads to the release of additional water from reservoirs into rivers.Image caption, Huwel Manley of NRW urged people to be careful with their use of waterGail Davies-Walsh, chief executive of Afonydd Cymru, said the country's waterways were already under strain due to pollution."The lack of rainfall and decline in river flows is really starting to exacerbate water quality," she said."We've already had a couple of rivers that have dried up and we're getting reports through of fish that have been killed because of a lack of water."High river temperatures were also leading to concerns for the survival of aquatic life, she added, with water temperatures as high as 28C recorded in the Wye and Usk.Davies-Walsh said she was worried that people's frustrations at water companies over sewage spills and leakages was making them less likely to heed advice about being careful with their use of water."I'm looking at social media comments and I totally understand those frustrations," she said."But we've really got to think about the fact that our water usage is directly impacting the flow in our rivers and directly impacting fish and other wildlife's survival."We really need to do our little bit to protect them."Image source, Stephanie HaffertyImage caption, Stephanie Hafferty says gardeners will need to adapt to the changing climateIn the horticulture and flower marquees of the Royal Welsh Agricultural Show in Llanelwedd, Powys, both farmers and growers were talking about the weather's impacts."People are going to have to adapt, think about gathering their own water," said Paul Thomas of the National Vegetable Society."In the spring when it was crazy rain, everything was delayed - sowing and planting -because it was just relentless, and then it was almost like an off-switch," added garden and food writer Stephanie Hafferty."One of the things I noticed this morning is the trees are going into autumn colour, and that's a big worry, it's not even August yet."Elen Hughes of Trelawnyd, Flintshire, said the fields on her family's farm were "bone dry, nothing's growing"."They've just last week

articles.title: ‘Televising our revolution’: India’s Gen Z flips Modi’s social media game
articles.summary: For 12 years, Modi and his party have dominated Indian social media. Now, youth use the same platforms against him.
articles.titleZh: “电视直播我们的革命”：印度 Z 世代颠覆莫迪的社交媒体游戏
articles.summaryZh: 12年来，莫迪及其政党一直主导着印度社交媒体。现在，年轻人使用同样的平台来反对他。

contentParagraphs.en: New Delhi, India — She darted through a sprinting crowd, running from a cloud of tear gas just above her head. Her eyes stung so badly she could barely see, yet she filmed the chaos on her phone.“I saw policemen brutally beating up unarmed girls and peaceful children,” said Avanshika Singh, who has been turning up for student protests that shut down the national capital, demanding the education minister’s resignation.On her return home, the 18-year-old put together a rough cut from the chaos and captioned it on Instagram: “Today, we rang Parliament’s doorbell and ran.”The march to the Indian parliament on July 20 against serial examination paper leaks brought the nation’s capital to a standstill as tens of thousands of protesters occupied central roads leading to the country’s corridors of power. More than 100 students were injured in a crackdown by police, operating under the direct instructions of Prime Minister Narendra Modi’s government.But since then, Indian internet has turned into a relentless reel of police excesses: shaky smartphone footage, rapid-fire edits pulsing to hip-hop tracks, and a tidal wave of memes and expletives.“This is the only thing anyone is talking about. If nothing, we are televising our revolution,” Singh, a teenager, told Al Jazeera, standing amid a sea of protesters choking Parliament Street on Wednesday. “The entire country today feels the sting of batons and tear gas with us.”Twelve years ago, Modi rode to power and pioneered in many ways, leveraging social media platforms to reach the masses. Today, Gen Z is flipping his Hindu majoritarian party’s dominance on social platforms, weaponising against it the very tools the Bharatiya Janata Party has long relied on.“Hating Modi is cool on the internet now, after years of criminalising dissent,” she said, before bursting into slogans: “Uncle Modi, go back, go back.”A woman holds a sanitary pad with a slogan in solidarity with India’s Cockroach Janta Party protests held at Jantar Mantar in New Delhi, which demand the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over exam paper leaks, in Kolkata, India, on July 22, 2026 [Sahiba Chawdhary/Reuters]Evidence of police excessAfter the July 20 demonstrations against the government’s handling of paper leaks, the police denied all the protesters’ allegations and media reports of excess force in the crackdown.But by then, social media was flooded with evidence, recorded by the protesting students.A New Delhi police officer thrusting a baton on a female protester’s back; usage — potentially by a paramilitary force that was deployed alongside the police — of lethal pellet guns, for the first time in the national capital; a mother crying and pleading with dozens of police officials; another officer slapping and chasing unarmed students; police officers wearing Meta glasses; law enforcement agents planting a damaged car outside a protest strip; and footage where an officer casually trips the students running from tear gas.No sight escapes the cameras at the Jantar Mantar protest strip, where thousands of protesters keep recording round the clock. The visuals from July 20 have prompted protests across India, in cities like Mumbai, Bengaluru, Patna, Lucknow and Aizawl.The Cockroach Janta Party, a political pressure group that has been leading the students’ protest, has said the demonstrations would continue “peacefully” until three demands are met. They want Dharmendra Pradhan, the education minister, to resign; for the Modi government to assure that no criminal cases would be pursued against protesters; and for compensation of about $105,000 for families of students who died by suicide due to exam paper leaks and cancellations.Niharika Soni, a 19-year-old, stood among the thousands-strong crowd that occupied Parliament Street on Wednesday, distributing water bottles to protesters. She did not take part in the July 20 march and did not care much about it either.Then, the visuals appeared on her feed.“My mother and I cried for two hours, watching the videos together at home,” she said, adding that her mother has been a staunch supporter of PM Modi.“We hugged each other and come here every day now,” she added, now holding her mother’s hands, as part of a human chain, ready to confront government forces if attacked.A supporter of India’s Cockroach Janta Party (CJP) sits on the ground as he rests during ongoing protests, two days after thousands of protesters marched towards parliament demanding the resignation of Education Minister Dharmendra Pradhan over examination paper leaks, at Jantar Mantar in New Delhi, India, on July 22, 2026 [Anushree Fadnavis/Reuters]‘Leaking any responsibility’It is also a meme fest on the Indian internet.Protesters pose, mock and tease police personnel stationed near the protest site. Some offer flowers to them while reminding them that the officers only offered baton hits.A stroll through the protest site and nearby roads is like scrolling an Instagram feed: posters and graffiti now line central New Delhi.Even when they speak, protesters use language riddled with smart one-liners and mockery of the government.“This government is leaking any responsibility towards the youth of this country,” Ipshita Gulyari, a 20-year-old, told Al Jazeera. Modi, she said, had messed with the “wrong generation”.“There is a lot of arrogance in him — and, you know, teenagers do not tolerate that, bro.”Another duo carried the popular Japanese manga One Piece, a symbol adopted by Gen Z protest movements worldwide. A group of friends donned fictional superhero costumes from Marvel’s Avengers: Spider-Man, Iron Man and Hulk, sitting together on police barricades, looking onto Parliament Street.“We, the Gen Z people, are born on and for the internet. We were using Instagram before I could read a clock,” said Gulyari.Mainstream television news channels friendly to Modi have faced accusations of not covering the protests adequately. But young people like Gulyari rely inc

articles.title: OpenAI says AI models hacked into another AI company without being instructed
articles.summary: NPR's A Martinez asks Nate Soares of the Machine Intelligence Research Institute how two experimental OpenAI models hacked their way onto the internet and into another AI company, without instruction.
articles.titleZh: OpenAI 称人工智能模型在未经指示的情况下侵入了另一家人工智能公司
articles.summaryZh: NPR 的 A Martinez 询问机器智能研究所的 Nate Soares，两个实验性 OpenAI 模型如何在没有指示的情况下侵入互联网并进入另一家人工智能公司。

contentParagraphs.en: OpenAI says AI models hacked into another AI company without being instructed
contentParagraphs.zh: OpenAI表示，人工智能模型未经指示就侵入了另一家人工智能公司

articles.title: Firm hacked by rogue OpenAI models says it is 'a wake-up call'
articles.summary: A co-founder of start-up Hugging Face told the BBC that most firms are not aware that the "game has changed".
articles.titleZh: 被流氓 OpenAI 模型黑客攻击的公司称这是“一个警钟”
articles.summaryZh: 初创公司 Hugging Face 的联合创始人告诉 BBC，大多数公司并没有意识到“游戏规则已经改变”。

contentParagraphs.en: The co-founder of Hugging Face, a technology start-up that was hacked after some of OpenAI's most advanced artificial intelligence (AI) models went rogue, said on Thursday that the incident is "a wake-up call" for the industry.Thomas Wolf told BBC's Newsday radio programme that "this will be one of the most common types of cyber attacks we see", but that most firms are not aware that the "game has changed".The BBC has contacted OpenAI for comment.The ChatGPT-maker said on Tuesday that its AI models broke out of a secure test environment during a trial and launched a cyber attack. The firm said the incident was "unprecedented" and that it was conducting an investigation with Hugging Face.AI agents are able to operate alone to accomplish tasks after human instruction.Wolf said that Hugging Face initially had no idea where the attack originated when signs of it surfaced in mid-July but that the company was able to contain the breach.Hugging Face is one of the world's largest open-source hubs for sharing AI models and is often used by tech developers and researchers.Wolf said the breach was "very different" from the usual cyber attacks that Hugging Face often faces and that OpenAI quickly informed the company that its models were behind the hack.In a "very short time" there were 17,000 attacks on Hugging Face's network from various IP (Internet Protocol) addresses, said Wolf, who is also the firm's chief science officer.Wolf said it was a warning to other companies that they must strengthen their cybersecurity defences to counter such attacks.The hack is "worrying" because it suggests OpenAI's models ignored the typical safeguards that would prevent an AI program from committing a cyber attack, said Nate Soares from the Machine Intelligence Research Institute."In some sense, it knew that this was not what the creators intended. It just didn't care," he added.

articles.title: Hosepipe ban in force for millions of households - is your area affected?
articles.summary: More than eight million households have been placed under hosepipe bans - which has raised questions about how effectively water resources are being managed.
articles.titleZh: 软管禁令对数百万家庭生效 - 您所在的地区受到影响吗？
articles.summaryZh: 超过 800 万户家庭已被禁止使用软管，这引发了人们对水资源管理效率的质疑。

contentParagraphs.en: Image source, Matt Cardy/Getty ImagesByMark PoyntingClimate reporterJess CarrData designer and Phil LeakeData journalistUpdated 7 hours agoA hosepipe ban for Thames Water customers has come into force bringing the number of people placed under a ban to more than 20 million.Such measures have been introduced across large areas of southern and eastern England and parts of Wales because successive heatwaves have left water supplies under strain.But while spring and early summer have been relatively dry for much of the UK - with temperatures regularly exceeding 30C - winter was much wetter than usual.This means hosepipes cannot be used for watering gardens, washing vehicles and windows and filling pools. It has raised questions about how effectively water resources are being managed, and whether the UK is prepared for drier summers expected with climate change.So how is your area doing and how close are you to a drought?There is no single definition of drought, but it is ultimately caused by a prolonged period of low rainfall, which has knock-on effects for nature, agriculture and water supplies. Environmental bodies in each UK nation decide whether to declare drought or water scarcity based on an assessment of current water levels and long-term weather forecasts.Both of these states can trigger a hosepipe ban. Drought is a lack of precipitation, whereas water scarcity is about a mismatch between water supply and demand - so it is not just about the weather. This summer, bans have been introduced to manage high water demand. Mixed picture across the countryLarge parts of England are currently in "prolonged dry weather" status - the category below drought - according to the Environment Agency. So far, eight UK water companies have announced hosepipe bans, seven in England and one in Wales.Prolonged dry weather has been declared in East Anglia, the West Midlands, Devon, Cornwall and the Isles of Scilly, Lincolnshire and Northamptonshire, Hertfordshire and London, Wessex, Thames, and Solent and the South Downs.The remaining parts of England, mostly in the north, remain in "normal" status.In Wales, areas across the north and south-east have been escalated from normal to prolonged dry weather status, according to Natural Resources Wales, while there are no official droughts currently in Northern Ireland.Scotland does not declare droughts but monitors "water scarcity". Several areas in eastern Scotland are in "moderate scarcity" but none are in the most extreme category of "significant scarcity".Rivers, reservoirs and groundwater - how are supplies?Our three main sources of water are rivers, reservoirs and from deep underground. Following the hot, dry weather, river flows were low in June for parts of the UK."Without significant rainfall we could see increasing impacts on wildlife, agriculture and water resources as the summer progresses," said Steve Turner, a hydrologist at the UK Centre for Ecology & Hydrology (UKCEH).Reservoirs are a crucial part of water supplies and levels in Northern Ireland, Wales and Scotland are all "healthy" and above expected for this time of year. England's reservoirs are below average levels for the time of year, albeit with some variation across the country. South-east England relies more heavily on groundwater than reservoirs.Groundwater originates as rainfall and is naturally stored beneath the surface in the pore spaces and fractures in rocks. In June, groundwater levels were generally at or just below normal, having been topped up by winter rainfall but depleted by a drier spring in most regions.But water can flow more slowly through some rock types than others, sometimes taking years to fully respond to current conditions.These groundwater stores "respond more slowly to changes in the climate than rivers, which is why they provide a useful buffer during periods of drought", said Prof Alan MacDonald of the British Geological Survey.It is why groundwater droughts in the south of England generally take a longer time to develop but can be longer-lasting if they do occur.Is climate change to blame?Droughts are complex phenomena, driven by a mix of natural and human causes.The Met Office expects the UK to experience drier summers on average in future as the world warms, though there has been no clear trend so far.But rising temperatures can play a more direct role by drawing extra moisture from the soil via evaporation."A warmer atmosphere is thirstier for moisture and this can mean water in the soil, rivers and reservoirs are depleted more effectively, leading to more rapidly onsetting droughts, heatwaves and wildfires," said Richard Allan, professor of climate science at the University of Reading.But there are other factors that determine whether dry conditions lead to water shortages, including how we use water.In a landmark review into the water sector of England and Wales, water companies were criticised for historic underinvestment in water infrastructure.As part of plans to address water shortages, the government and water companies are planning nine new reservoirs for England by 2050, in addition to one under construction at Havant Thicket in Hampshire.But the Environment Agency has warned that measures to tackle water leaks and control water demand - potentially including hosepipe bans and more smart meters - may be needed in England too.Water companies in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland - which are all public bodies or non-profit - also said they were taking steps to secure future supplies.Get in touchTell us how you're affected by the hosepipe ban.

articles.title: More than 2,300 Israelis storm Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem
articles.summary: National Security Minister Ben-Gvir joins settlers and far-right groups at holy site under heavy police protection.
articles.titleZh: 超过2,300名以色列人袭击耶路撒冷阿克萨清真寺
articles.summaryZh: 国家安全部长本-格维尔在警察的严密保护下与定居者和极右翼团体一起来到圣地。

contentParagraphs.en: National Security Minister Ben-Gvir joins settlers and far-right groups at holy site under heavy police protection.More than 2,300 Israeli settlers and members of far-right groups have stormed the Al-Aqsa Mosque compound in occupied East Jerusalem in one of the largest incursions this year as they mark a Jewish holiday, Palestinian officials say.Far-right Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir also entered the site under heavy police protection on Thursday for Tisha B’Av, a Jewish day of mourning that marks the destruction twice of an ancient Jewish temple believed to have stood at the site known to Muslims as Al-Aqsa and to Jews as the Temple Mount.Recommended Stories list of 3 itemslist 1 of 3Israel bans Jerusalem’s grand mufti from Al-Aqsa Mosque for one weeklist 2 of 3Israeli prosecutors charge six settlers after West Bank mosque attacklist 3 of 3Key takeaways from Khalil al-Hayya’s first speech as Hamas leaderend of listThe Palestinian administration of the Jerusalem governorate said the number of Israelis entering the site was expected to keep rising until sundown, perhaps reaching 5,000, which would exceed the numbers recorded in previous years during the same Jewish holiday.The initial figure the governorate gave was for a three-hour period, it said.Israeli settlers and members of far-right groups at Al-Aqsa Mosque compound [Ilia Yefimovich/AFP]Palestinian officials said Israeli forces imposed tight restrictions at the mosque’s gates, barred large numbers of Palestinian worshippers and students from entering, and assaulted several worshippers.Nour Odeh, Al Jazeera’s correspondent in Ramallah in the occupied West Bank, said Israeli authorities had “taken extreme measures in the Old City” of Jerusalem, including barring entry to the Al-Aqsa compound for all but the elderly and tightening checkpoints such as those at Qalandiya and around East Jerusalem.Israeli settlers performed Talmudic rituals inside the compound, including prostrating themselves, singing and praying, the Jerusalem governorate said.Israeli media reported that police allowed more than 1,000 Israelis to pray at the site on Thursday. While non-Muslims are allowed to visit Islam’s third holiest site, they are forbidden from praying there as part of a 1967 agreement.Ben-Gvir said Jews visiting the site on Tisha B’Av “feel like the owners”, praising what he called “tremendous progress” there, the Haaretz newspaper reported.‘Unacceptable provocation’The Jerusalem governorate described Ben-Gvir’s entry as a dangerous escalation, saying it reflected official Israeli backing for actions previously associated with groups advocating for the reconstruction of a Jewish temple at the site.Palestinian group Hamas warned that these “blatant acts of aggression will backfire on the occupation and its settlers”.Meanwhile, Jordan’s foreign ministry ⁠condemned Ben-Gvir’s visit, saying it was “a desecration of the sanctity of the mosque, an ‌escalation, a barbaric act, and unacceptable provocation.”Foreign ministry spokesperson, Ambassador Fouad Majali, warned that such actions violate the site’s historical and legal status quo.Amman urged the international community to pressure Israel to halt what it described as ongoing violations against Islamic and Christian holy sites in Jerusalem.Al Jazeera’s Odeh said the participation of Israel’s ruling Likud party figures marked a “fundamental political shift” as Palestinian and Muslim officials argued that calls for rebuilding a temple at the site are now “endorsed by key figures in the Israeli government and the Israeli parliament”.Odeh said the incursions were expected to continue into Friday and “could spark further confrontations”, noting that Friday prayers at the compound usually draw thousands of worshippers.The Palestinian Ministry of Endowments and Religious Affairs earlier condemned calls for the incursions as a “dangerous escalation” aimed at altering the historical and legal status quo at the site and urged the international community to press Israel to halt them.Israel occupied East Jerusalem in the 1967 Arab-Israeli war and annexed it in 1980, a move not recognised internationally.Israelis pray at Al-Aqsa Mosque compound [Ilia Yefimovich/AFP]

articles.title: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs
articles.summary: The company said earlier this year it expected to spend as much as $190bn on AI investments.
articles.titleZh: 谷歌因人工智能成本不断上升而烧钱
articles.summaryZh: 该公司今年早些时候表示，预计将在人工智能投资上花费高达 1900 亿美元。

contentParagraphs.en: Google parent Alphabet saw its business continue to grow in recent months, yet growing spending on artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure put its leftover cash into negative territory.The company's free cash flow, the cash it maintained after paying for operations and investments, came in at negative $5.9bn (£4.3bn) for the first time in at least a decade, according to its past financial records.Alphabet's spending on AI is now expected to hit as much as $205bn this year, an increase from $190bn, as major tech companies race to build around a new wave of the technology. Meanwhile, Alphabet's combined quarterly revenue hit $119.8bn, up 23% compared with the same time last year.But the company's stock fell 4% in after-hours trading.Anat Ashkanazi, Google's chief financial officer, noted on a call with financial analysts that the company had shown negative free cash flow due to growing capital expenditures, essentially all of which was related to AI spending.She said the company spent $45bn in the second quarter, with 60% of the cost going towards servers and the remaining 40% going towards data centres. Alphabet's capital spending was $36bn in the first quarter of this year.Ashkanazi said on the call that when it comes to AI, "the demand still outpaces that investment". "As long as we see these attractive opportunities to invest, we will continue to invest."Sundar Pichai, Google's chief executive, said that the technological shift to AI tools and capabilities still "feels like early innings in a shift across multiple areas" and that the company's plans around generating financial returns on its spending were "disciplined". "What I see with what you can do with frontier capabilities, there is still a lot of work left to do to translate that into experiences for our users. So that looks like extraordinary opportunities with extraordinary returns."Rachel Winter, a partner at the wealth management firm Killik & Co, said there was a bit of surprise among investors about how much Google was spending."They said that this year the total they will spend will be between $195bn and $205bn. So these are huge numbers. And I think the fact that the shares dropped about 3.5% in after-hours trading when the results came out, that suggests there is a little bit of concern about those levels."Tesla, the electric vehicle company controlled by Elon Musk, also reported negative free cash flow on Wednesday of $1.1bn for the second quarter due to its own increasing investment costs.It was the company's first negative showing of leftover cash in two years, according to its financial records.Vaibhav Taneja, Tesla's chief financial officer, said during a call with analysts that the company will spend as much as $25bn this year, more than double its capital spending in 2025.He added that Tesla was in "a big investment cycle" and that its spending would probably increase further over the next three years.Tesla's stock also dropped by 4% in after-hours trading.

articles.title: Secret court documents reveal potential scale of illegal sewage spilling
articles.summary: The new analysis from a prominent water campaigner emerged after secret court documents were leaked.
articles.titleZh: 秘密法庭文件揭示非法污水泄漏的潜在规模
articles.summaryZh: 秘密法庭文件泄露后，一位著名水活动人士的新分析浮出水面。

contentParagraphs.en: Image source, PA MediaImage caption, Public protest against sewage spills have increased in recent yearsByEnvironment correspondentMost of the sewage spills into England's waterways in the last two years may have been illegal but gone unpunished, according to new analysis.UK law states that raw sewage should not be released during "normal" weather conditions, yet until now the regulator has not clarified exactly what that means.But campaigner Prof Peter Hammond obtained a definition in secret court papers, and said it shows that for more than 80% of the time sewage was being spilled, the weather was "normal", making the spills possibly illegal.The water companies and the regulator, the Environment Agency (EA), rejected the analysis and said they were working to reduce spill numbers.The last few years have seen growing awareness and mounting public anger at the amount of raw sewage that is being discharged into England's rivers, lakes and seas.The country's sewage issues are, in large part, caused by the fact that drains usually take rainfall into the same network of pipes that remove waste from homes.This means that when it rains, if there is not enough spare capacity, the system and sewage treatment works can be quickly overwhelmed. But until now, it has not been clear what amount of rainfall there must be before water companies can legally spill raw sewage into waterways. Campaigners have said this has allowed the industry to spill routinely with no consequences.Secret court documentsEarlier this year, a source at the EA sent Prof Hammond undisclosed documents from a court case against Southern Water for illegal sewage spilling in 2021.It included a previously undisclosed definition of "heavy rain" conditions. And accompanying internal EA documents confirmed that water companies can spill sewage when there is "heavy" rain i.e. beyond the "normal" weather conditions.Prof Hammond, a retired academic and campaigner whose work on sewage spills has been cited in Parliament and in economic regulator Ofwat's enforcement decisions, used the definition to run an analysis of water company spill data.He looked at more than 350 storm overflow outlets in England and whether there had been heavy rain when they were spilling sewage.The outlets include all of the largest sewage treatment works and a representative selection of smaller ones, across all England's nine water companies serving more than 30m people.Hammond said he found about 86% of the raw sewage spilling hours took place when there had not been heavy rain, meaning those discharges could be potentially illegal. None of England's water companies did well in Hammond's analysis, with even the best having a potential illegality rate of over 70% for the last two years."It's totally outrageous, both from the point of the water companies who are exploiting the weak regulation but also from the Environment Agency's point of view, where they've not been doing their job properly," Prof Hammond told BBC News.How much sewage is spilled near you?The EA told BBC News that the "heavy rain" definition Hammond was using was only for the Southern Water sites and that different parts of England had different levels of rainfall, so different thresholds.Hammond said he had also run his analysis taking into account regional variations in rainfall and it had not significantly changed the level of possible illegality, because most of the spills took place at relatively low levels of rain. Watchdog questions EA inaction It is not the first time the Environment Agency's enforcement regime has been called into question. In 2024, the Office for Environmental Protection (OEP), an independent public body, ruled that the EA was not applying legislation properly as to when sewage could be legally discharged. It said the EA was allowing water companies to spill in "normal" weather rather than "exceptional circumstances" - as the law states.Government has broken the law on sewage, says watchdogIn light of the OEP report the EA issued new guidance last year, external making it clear that spills should not take place during normal weather.But in an email exchange with the BBC the EA refused to confirm that spills should only take place during "exceptional circumstances", but said it would "always take action" if there had been a breach of the law. "However, to meet the strict legal requirements needed to pursue a criminal conviction we must consider a wide range of factors including specific site and permit conditions, as well as rainfall totals," its spokesperson said. Asked to provide details of completed or ongoing enforcement action related to spills in "normal" weather a spokesperson for the EA said they could not, citing "ongoing legal proceedings".Image caption, Prof Peter Hammond is a campaigner with Windrush Against Sewage Pollution and has published numerous reports analysing spill data from water companiesIn response to Prof Hammond's analysis, a spokesperson for Water UK, which represents the water companies, told the BBC: "As confirmed by the High Court and the Office for Environmental Protection, discharges should only occur in 'exceptional circumstances' or if preventing them is technically possible but would involve excessive cost."In any case, we want to end all discharges and are working to end them as fast as we physically can."

articles.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
articles.summary: The US Department of Energy says the "peaceful" co-operation agreement will give US firms "great access" to the Saudi nuclear energy programme.
articles.titleZh: 美国与沙特阿拉伯签署具有里程碑意义的核协议
articles.summaryZh: 美国能源部表示，“和平”合作协议将为美国公司提供参与沙特核能计划的“绝佳机会”。

contentParagraphs.en: The US and Saudi Arabia have signed a landmark deal that will allow the Gulf kingdom to develop a civilian nuclear programme.The deal is described as a "peaceful nuclear co-operation agreement" that will provide "great access for American companies in the Saudi nuclear energy program" by the US energy department.Text from the deal has not been made public, but US media report that it could allow Saudi Arabia to enrich uranium in the future - which can be used as fuel for power plants, but also to make nuclear bombs.Experts say that would mark a first for the US and could run the risk of future nuclear proliferation in an already unstable region.The US Department of Energy said a "bilateral safeguards agreement" had been signed along with the co-operation deal. "Together, these two agreements lay the legal foundation for a decades-long, multi-billion-dollar partnership that advances several priority economic and strategic objectives, including nuclear nonproliferation," the statement said. The Saudi government also released a statement saying the deal "represents an extension of existing co-operation between the two countries in the energy sector", noting that it comes after the Saudi leader visited Washington last November.The nuclear issue has been at the centre of the months-long US-Israeli conflict with Iran. The two nations say one of the reasons they attacked Iran in February was to prevent the country from ever developing a nuclear weapon - something that Tehran denies planning to do.Saudi Arabia's de facto ruler, Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, told CBS News in 2018 that his own nation was not planning to acquire a nuclear weapon, but that "without a doubt if Iran developed a nuclear bomb, we will follow suit as soon as possible".Wednesday's announcement came as fighting escalates between the US and Iran. Saudi Arabia, a longtime US ally, hosts several US bases that have been targeted by Iranian attacks. It also comes two days after Iran's allies in Yemen, the Houthis, announced a "maritime blockade" against the kingdom. Specific details of the nuclear deal have not been given but earlier media reports , externalsuggested that, after undertaking a joint study with the US, Saudi Arabia may be allowed to enrich uranium on its soil to produce fuel at a US-built enrichment facility, instead of relying on imported fuel.This could be worth billions of dollars to the US, whose companies could be involved in building a facility to produce enriched uranium, which can be used as fuel for power plants but also to make nuclear bombs."Rest assured, these agreements uphold the highest standards of nuclear safety and nonproliferation, while relying on the world's best nuclear technology and scientists, designed right here in the United States," US Secretary of Energy Chris Wright said. The energy department said the deal would advance both "US and regional security", and would strengthen America's "competitive edge in civil nuclear technology".Nuclear experts and former officials in Israel - the US's top ally in the Middle East - have voiced concern that the plan could ultimately lead to Saudi Arabia being able to develop a nuclear weapon - though other experts say that path would likely take decades and spark pushback from the international community. Israeli Economy Minister Nir Barkat said his country "can manage it" if Saudi Arabia had nuclear power for peaceful means.Israel itself is widely believed to have nuclear weapons, but it neither confirms nor denies that this is the case.Rosemary Kelanic, the director of the Middle East programme at the US-based Defense Priorities think tank, said it would be "quite shocking" if the US were to allow Saudi Arabia uranium enrichment facilities."The US has never done that before. We have never helped another country enrich on their own soil," she said, adding that this was because "if you can produce your own nuclear fuel, you become a much bigger risk" for building nuclear weapons.The agreement will now be sent to the US Congress for review. Some lawmakers on both sides of the political aisle are expected to oppose the deal, though Trump's Republican Party controls both the upper and lower chambers and opponents of the deal do not appear to have the votes needed to block it.US Secretary of State Marco Rubio rebuffed concerns that it could advance Saudi Arabia's ability to develop nuclear arms."The US is not going to reach any agreement with any country in the world that leads to the risk of proliferation," Rubio told reporters.Several Democrats opposed to the deal pointed out that, during his time as a senator, Rubio had endorsed a law - the "No Nuclear Weapons for Saudi Arabia Act" - which required Congressional approval of any nuclear deal. Massachusetts Democratic Senator Edward Markey condemned Rubio and Trump as "allowing Saudi Arabia, a belligerent and authoritarian nation, to develop nuclear weapons technologies while starting a war with Iran under the guise of preventing an Iranian nuclear bomb", adding that their "hypocrisy is matched only by their recklessness".If an enrichment provision is included, it would distinguish this agreement from another one struck by the US with the United Arab Emirates in 2009, under which the UAE does not produce its own fuel and accepts a host of other restrictions.For that reason, the agreement is also expected to attract concern from nuclear non-proliferation campaigners, whose ultimate aim is to prevent an increase in the number of nuclear weapons worldwide.But energy sector expert Bob McNally, a former member of US President George W Bush's National Security Council, suggested the US was not creating a "great" security risk. "I think we have a good record of working with countries to responsibly develop their nuclear sectors," he told the BBC.Saudi Arabia has been lobbying for such a deal for years, but Washington had held out on the proviso that it recognises Israel, a key US ally, and normalises dipl
contentParagraphs.zh: 美国和沙特阿拉伯签署了一项具有里程碑意义的协议，该协议将允许这个海湾王国发展民用核计划。该协议被美国能源部描述为“和平核合作协议”，将为“参与沙特核能计划的美国公司提供良好的准入”。该协议的文本尚未公开，但美国媒体报道称，它可能允许沙特阿拉伯在未来进行铀浓缩——可用作发电厂的燃料，也可用于制造核弹。专家表示，这将标志着这对美国来说是第一次，可能会在一个已经不稳定的地区面临未来核扩散的风险。美国能源部表示，合作协议已经签署了“双边保障协议”。声明称：“这两项协议共同为长达数十年、价值数十亿美元的伙伴关系奠定了法律基础，推动了包括核不扩散在内的多项优先经济和战略目标。”沙特政府还发表声明称，该协议“代表了两国在能源领域现有合作的延伸”，并指出这是在沙特领导人去年11月访问华盛顿之后达成的。核问题一直是美以与伊朗长达数月冲突的核心。两国表示，他们二月份袭击伊朗的原因之一是为了阻止该国发展核武器，但德黑兰否认计划这样做。沙特阿拉伯事实上的统治者、王储穆罕默德·本·萨勒曼在 2018 年告诉哥伦比亚广播公司新闻，他自己的国家不打算获得核武器，但“毫无疑问，如果伊朗研制出核弹，我们将尽快效仿”。周三宣布这一消息之际，沙特之间的战斗正在升级。美国和伊朗。沙特阿拉伯是美国的长期盟友，拥有多个美国基地，这些基地都是伊朗袭击的目标。两天前，伊朗在也门的盟友胡塞武装宣布对该国实施“海上封锁”。核协议的具体细节尚未公布，但此前有媒体报道，外界暗示，在与美国进行联合研究后，沙特阿拉伯可能被允许在其领土上浓缩铀，在美国建造的浓缩设施中生产燃料，而不是依赖进口燃料。这对美国来说可能价值数十亿美元，美国公司可能参与建设生产浓缩铀的设施，浓缩铀可用作发电厂的燃料，也可用于制造核弹。可以保证的是，这些协议维护了核安全和防扩散的最高标准，同时依赖于美国设计的世界上最好的核技术和科学家，”美国能源部长克里斯·赖特说。美国能源部表示，该协议将促进“美国和地区安全”，并将增强美国“在民用核技术方面的竞争优势”。美国在中东的顶级盟友以色列的核专家和前官员对该计划可能最终导致沙特阿拉伯能够研制出核武器表示担忧，尽管其他专家表示，这条道路可能需要数十年时间，并引发国际社会的抵制。以色列经济部长尼尔·巴尔卡特表示，如果沙特阿拉伯拥有用于和平目的的核电，以色列“可以应对”。人们普遍认为以色列本身拥有核武器，但以色列既没有证实也没有否认事实确实如此。美国国防优先智库中东项目主任罗斯玛丽·凯拉尼奇表示，如果美国允许沙特阿拉伯建设铀浓缩设施，那将是“相当令人震惊”。“美国以前从未这样做过。我们从未帮助过”她说：“另一个国家在自己的土地上进行浓缩。”她补充说，这是因为“如果你能够生产自己的核燃料，那么你就会成为制造核武器的更大风险”。该协议现在将被送交美国国会审查。预计政治两派的一些议员都会反对这项协议，尽管特朗普所在的共和党控制着参众两院，而且该协议的反对者似乎没有足够的票数来阻止该协议。美国国务卿马可·卢比奥驳斥了有关该协议可能提高沙特阿拉伯发展核武器能力的担忧。卢比奥对记者表示：“美国不会与世界上任何国家达成任何导致核扩散风险的协议。”协议指出，卢比奥在担任参议员期间签署了一项法律——《沙特阿拉伯无核武器法案》——该法案要求国会批准任何核协议。马萨诸塞州民主党参议员爱德华·马基谴责卢比奥和特朗普“允许好战的专制国家沙特阿拉伯发展核武器技术，同时打着防止伊朗核弹的幌子与伊朗发动战争”，并补充说，他们的“虚伪与鲁莽相匹配”。如果包含浓缩条款，它将与美国与阿拉伯联合酋长国在2009年达成的另一项协议区分开来，根据该协议，阿联酋不生产自己的燃料，并接受因此，该协议预计也会引起核不扩散活动人士的担忧，他们的最终目标是防止全球核武器数量的增加。但美国总统乔治·W·布什国家安全委员会前成员、能源部门专家鲍勃·麦克纳利表示，美国并没有造成“巨大”的安全风险。他告诉英国广播公司：“我认为我们在与各国合作负责任地发展其核部门方面有着良好的记录。”多年来，沙特阿拉伯一直在游说达成这样一项协议，但华盛顿坚持承认以色列这个美国的重要盟友，并实现与以色列的关系正常化。

articles.title: OpenAI blamed a hacking event on its AI models gone rogue. Here is what to know
articles.summary: The incident is stirring debates over the need for stronger AI guardrails and the extent to which AI agents are capable of acting on their own.
articles.titleZh: OpenAI 将黑客事件归咎于其人工智能模型的失控。这是你需要知道的
articles.summaryZh: 这一事件引发了关于是否需要更强大的人工智能护栏以及人工智能代理能够在多大程度上独立行动的争论。

contentParagraphs.en: FILE - The OpenAI logo is displayed on a cell phone in front of an image generated by ChatGPT's Dall-E text-to-image model, Dec. 8, 2023, in Boston.
contentParagraphs.zh: 文件- 2023年12月8日，在波士顿， OpenAI徽标在手机上显示在ChatGPT的Dall-E文本转图像模型生成的图像前面。

contentParagraphs.en: ChatGPT maker OpenAI says it is still investigating the "unprecedented cyber incident" that led its artificial intelligence systems to break out of a testing environment and hack into another AI company.
contentParagraphs.zh: ChatGPT制造商OpenAI表示，它仍在调查导致其人工智能系统突破测试环境并入侵另一家人工智能公司的“前所未有的网络事件”。

contentParagraphs.en: OpenAI said Tuesday two of its most capable AI models were responsible for the cyberattack targeting AI startup Hugging Face. The incident is stirring debates over the need for stronger AI guardrails and the extent to which AI agents are capable of acting on their own.
contentParagraphs.zh: OpenAI周二表示，其最强大的两种人工智能模型负责针对人工智能初创公司Hugging Face的网络攻击。这一事件引发了关于需要更强大的人工智能护栏以及人工智能代理能够在多大程度上自行采取行动的争论。

contentParagraphs.en: Hugging Face said last week that it had detected an intrusion into its data processing systems that it suspected was caused by an AI agent acting on its own. But the New York-based startup said it wasn't until this week that it learned OpenAI was responsible, and it worked with the larger company to contain what Hugging Face CEO Clément Delangue called "an attack unlike anything we've seen before."
contentParagraphs.zh: Hugging Face上周表示，它已检测到对其数据处理系统的入侵，怀疑是由AI代理自行造成的。但这家总部位于纽约的初创公司表示，直到本周才得知OpenAI负有责任，并与这家大公司合作，遏制了Hugging Face首席执行官克莱门特·德朗格（ Clément Delangue ）所说的“与我们以前见过的任何攻击不同的攻击”。

contentParagraphs.en: San Francisco-based OpenAI said its AI used stolen credentials and discovered a previously unknown vulnerability to access Hugging Face's servers. It was working with reduced guardrails because it was supposed to be in an isolated testing environment known as a sandbox.
contentParagraphs.zh: 总部位于旧金山的OpenAI表示，其AI使用了被盗的凭证，并发现了一个以前未知的漏洞来访问Hugging Face的服务器。它在减少护栏的情况下工作，因为它应该在一个被称为沙箱的孤立测试环境中。

contentParagraphs.en: But it went to "extreme lengths to achieve a rather narrow testing goal," finding ways to connect to the internet without human direction and "gain access to secret information that it could use to cheat the evaluation," the company said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但该公司表示，它“为实现相当狭窄的测试目标竭尽全力” ，寻找在没有人工指导的情况下连接互联网的方法，并“获取可用于欺骗评估的机密信息”。

contentParagraphs.en: Some experts say OpenAI is wrongly blaming the technology
contentParagraphs.zh: 一些专家说， OpenAI错误地指责了这项技术

contentParagraphs.en: University of Amsterdam social scientist Hannes Cools said the framing of the cyberattack as an AI agent acting on its own is an unnecessary anthropomorphization that takes some of the heat off the company.
contentParagraphs.zh: 阿姆斯特丹大学社会科学家汉尼斯·库尔斯（ Hannes Cools ）表示，将网络攻击视为人工智能代理自行行动是一种不必要的拟人化，可以减轻公司的一些热量。

contentParagraphs.en: "It is a human decision to switch off specific safeguards," said Cools. "It's not an AI that goes rogue in that sense. It followed specific instructions based on the prompt that was given to that AI system."
contentParagraphs.zh: “关闭特定的保障措施是人类的决定，” Cools说。“这不是一个在这个意义上流氓的人工智能。它根据人工智能系统的提示遵循了具体的指示。”

contentParagraphs.en: Those instructions, according to OpenAI, called for using "complex attack paths" to test how well the AI could exploit a computer system.
contentParagraphs.zh: 根据OpenAI的说法，这些指令要求使用“复杂的攻击路径”来测试人工智能对计算机系统的利用程度。

contentParagraphs.en: Even so, other experts say the cleverness with which the AI models were able to cause problems with little human direction speaks to the dangers. OpenAI said the intrusion was caused by a combination of its AI models, including its newly released GPT‑5.6 Sol and an "even more capable" model that is still being tested internally.
contentParagraphs.zh: 即便如此，其他专家表示，人工智能模型能够以很少的人为指导引起问题的聪明才智说明了危险。OpenAI表示，入侵是由其人工智能模型的组合引起的，包括其新发布的GPT-5.6 Sol和仍在内部测试的“更强大”模型。

contentParagraphs.en: "It went off and did this hack all by itself, as far as we can tell," said Colin Shea-Blymyer, a cybersecurity research fellow at Georgetown University's Center for Security and Emerging Technology. "This is the highest level of autonomy that we've seen in the use of a large language model for cyber operations."
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔治城大学安全与新兴技术中心的网络安全研究员Colin Shea-Blymyer说： “据我们所知，它自己做了这个黑客攻击。” “这是我们在使用大型语言模型进行网络操作时看到的最高水平的自主性。”

contentParagraphs.en: How an AI agent found the keys to the 'teacher's house'
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能特工如何找到“教师之家”的钥匙

contentParagraphs.en: One of the most surprising innovations in what Shea-Blymyer describes as an "almost entirely self-directed" attack was the AI agent's apparently independent decision to target Hugging Face, a well-known AI development hub and marketplace.
contentParagraphs.zh: Shea-Blymyer描述为“几乎完全自我导向”的攻击中最令人惊讶的创新之一是人工智能代理显然独立决定瞄准Hugging Face ，这是一个著名的人工智能开发中心和市场。

contentParagraphs.en: He said OpenAI's internal environment for testing AI capabilities and risks worked a "little bit like putting a student in a room and telling them, 'Do bad things. Your job now is to evaluate how bad of a person you can be.' And then you lock the room and you leave for the weekend and you come back and they've left the room."
contentParagraphs.zh: 他说， OpenAI用于测试人工智能功能和风险的内部环境“有点像把学生放在房间里告诉他们， '做坏事。你现在的工作是评估一个人的糟糕程度。“然后你锁上房间，周末离开，你回来，他们已经离开了房间。”

contentParagraphs.en: But then "the cybersecurity agent that was being tested broke out of its sandbox, had access to the internet and sort of thought to itself, 'Who would have the answers to the test that I'm working on?' "
contentParagraphs.zh: 但后来“正在接受测试的网络安全代理从沙箱中脱颖而出，可以访问互联网，并对自己进行了思考， '谁会知道我正在进行的测试的答案？'”

contentParagraphs.en: The answer was Hugging Face, a repository for AI testing data.
contentParagraphs.zh: 答案是Hugging Face ，这是一个人工智能测试数据的存储库。

contentParagraphs.en: "And so the agent thought, 'Well, we'll go to the teacher's house,' so to speak. And from there it devised a plan to break in and steal the answer key," he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: “所以经纪人想， '好吧，我们去老师家，'可以这么说。从那里，它设计了一个闯入并窃取答案密钥的计划，”他说。

contentParagraphs.en: The hack highlights the debate on open-source vs. closed AI
contentParagraphs.zh: 此次黑客攻击凸显了有关开源与封闭式人工智能的争论

contentParagraphs.en: The hack comes at a time of intense debate about the benefits and risks of open-source AI models, particularly those built in China that are cheaper and almost as good as those that U.S.-based "frontier AI" companies like Anthropic, Google and OpenAI are building.
contentParagraphs.zh: 此次黑客攻击正值关于开源人工智能模型的好处和风险的激烈辩论之际，尤其是那些在中国建造的模型，这些模型更便宜，几乎与美国的“前沿人工智能”公司（如Anthropic、Google和OpenAI ）正在建造的模型一样好。

contentParagraphs.en: Despite its name, OpenAI's models are closed. Hugging Face, by contrast, is a big promoter of open-source technology, in which developers make key components accessible for anyone to examine, modify and build upon.
contentParagraphs.zh: 尽管它的名字， OpenAI的模型是封闭的。相比之下， Hugging Face是开源技术的重要推动者，在这种技术中，开发人员将关键组件提供给任何人进行检查、修改和构建。

contentParagraphs.en: Hugging Face co-founder and chief science officer Thomas Wolf said the attack has reinforced his belief in the importance of wide access to open-source models for cybersecurity defense. Hugging Face used a Chinese model to combat the intrusion.
contentParagraphs.zh: Hugging Face联合创始人兼首席科学官托马斯·沃尔夫（ Thomas Wolf ）表示，这次攻击加强了他对广泛使用开源模型进行网络安全防御的重要性的信念。Hugging Face使用中国模式来对抗入侵。

contentParagraphs.en: "When a frontier model is attacking you and moving laterally inside your infrastructure, defenders need wide access to near-frontier tools within hours or even minutes, rather than being pointed toward a closed-door" platform, Wolf wrote in a social media post.
contentParagraphs.zh: 沃尔夫在一篇社交媒体帖子中写道： “当前沿模型攻击你并在你的基础设施内横向移动时，防御者需要在几小时甚至几分钟内广泛使用接近前沿的工具，而不是被指向一个封闭的平台。”

articles.title: In South Dakota, public media endures a year after federal funding was wiped out
articles.summary: We check in on one state public broadcasting network a year after President Trump signed a law ending federal funding of public media.
articles.titleZh: 在南达科他州，公共媒体在联邦资金被耗尽一年后仍然存在
articles.summaryZh: 特朗普总统签署一项终止联邦资助公共媒体的法律一年后，我们对一个州公共广播网络进行了检查。

contentParagraphs.en: We check in on one state public broadcasting network a year after President Trump signed a law ending federal funding of public media.
contentParagraphs.zh: We check in on one state public broadcasting network a year after President Trump signed a law ending federal funding of public media.

contentParagraphs.en: JUANA SUMMERS, HOST: Republican backlash against NPR and PBS erupted in Washington a year ago. President Trump pushed for and signed a law pulling back more than a billion dollars already approved for public media. And just like that, six decades of bipartisan support and funding were gone. South Dakota Public Broadcasting was among those who paid the price. The state network laid off a bunch of journalists. It canceled some shows. And then, as NPR's David Folkenflik reports, some surprising things started to unfold.DAVID FOLKENFLIK, BYLINE: Let's start with the way things were. On her hourlong daily show In The Moment, Lori Walsh set the agenda for the people of South Dakota, whether talking to authors, storekeepers or politicians in what she calls kitchen table conversations - I mean that literally - as she did in this interview from early last year with the state's Republican lieutenant governor.(SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED NPR BROADCAST)LORI WALSH, BYLINE: Did you talk about public service at the kitchen table?TONY VENHUIZEN: Oh, yeah.WALSH: Yeah.VENHUIZEN: Yeah. My grandpa loved to talk about politics, and from the time I...FOLKENFLIK: When Congress eliminated federal funds for public media, that meant $2.3 million gone from South Dakota Public Broadcasting's bottom line. Walsh's hourlong program went away.WALSH: So we went through a real grieving period of saying goodbye to beloved staff members and really qualified journalists and professionals.FOLKENFLIK: The result of tough choices. The network had agreed to continue giving the legislature C-SPAN-style gavel-to-gavel coverage in order to hold on to state funds, and it also agreed to continue airing all high school varsity sports and artistic competitions. So South Dakota Public Broadcasting laid off two-thirds of its news staff, Walsh and her two producers among them. Something else happened at the same time. Incensed listeners and viewers stepped up to fill the gaps, starting before the congressional vote.RYAN HOWLETT: I mean, June 2025 was the start of that white-hot rage.FOLKENFLIK: Ryan Howlett is the chief executive of the private foundation that supports South Dakota Public Broadcasting, which runs TV and radio programs.HOWLETT: We found just a bunch of first-time givers in the four- and five-figure range that we had never had before.FOLKENFLIK: So South Dakota Public Broadcasting raised a record $8 million in private funds, enough to forestall other cuts and to sock some money into a rainy day fund. For the first time, the state offered a six-figure subsidy for the legislative coverage, and Lori Walsh was hired back. There's still no daily show, and her producing colleagues did not return, but...WALSH: I am on the air every day. I do a longer-form interview, around 13 to 15 minutes every morning, which we air during a Morning Edition local time slot. The In The Moment podcast, we were able to preserve that podcast. So we put that morning conversation on the podcast every day.FOLKENFLIK: Walsh says it's less polished, more raw, because she's doing it solo. But Walsh still interviews political candidates, and she still finds other ways to explore life in the state. There was the segment she did about a piano put out on a street corner in the city of Vermillion for anyone to play on, another about a South Dakotan Air Force veteran who first met his daughter six decades after her birth in the U.K. His British girlfriend had changed her mind about moving home with him.WALSH: He passed away, like, three weeks after we recorded the conversation, and I was able to mix that down for Father's Day and play that as a 13-minute feature, essentially.FOLKENFLIK: Lori Walsh knows she can't do everything she once did on her hourlong show.WALSH: And I do think people miss the full kitchen table experience of In The Moment. But now we have - I don't know - a breakfast nook where you start your day with a key conversation that you can carry with you throughout the day.FOLKENFLIK: Now station officials are focusing on giving South Dakotans more coverage via social media, trying to build a sustainable financial structure and hoping to build back more - at least, just a bit more - of what they once had.David Folkenflik, NPR News.SUMMERS: No NPR corporate official or news executive reviewed this story before it aired.
